findings.description: Path traversal risk: report_path from state is passed to Path() and used to read files. If report_path is user-controlled and unvalidated upstream, an attacker could read arbitrary files (e.g., ../../etc/passwd). Currently mitigated by: (1) authorization node runs first, (2) CLI interface controls path input, (3) use_case.execute validates Trivy JSON structure.
findings.fix: Add path validation in ingest_node (e.g., resolve and check against allowed base directory) or ensure CLI/API layer validates paths before they enter state. Current risk is LOW in practice due to existing mitigations.

findings.description: Exception message includes raw error details via f-string interpolation (f"Enrichment failed: {e}"). If pipeline errors are surfaced to users (e.g., in Phase 7 Streamlit dashboard or API responses), internal details (stack traces, file paths, connection strings) could leak.
findings.fix: Use a generic user-facing message and log the full exception separately. The structlog call on line 91 already logs the details — the error list in state should contain sanitized messages only.

findings.description: Same pattern as enrich_node: f"Escalation failed: {e}" stores raw exception in state errors list. Could leak internal details if displayed to users.
findings.fix: Replace with generic message in state; detailed error already logged via structlog.

findings.description: Same pattern: f"Ingestion failed: {e}" stores raw exception in state errors list.
findings.fix: Replace with generic message in state; detailed error already logged via structlog.

summary.notes: No CRITICAL or HIGH findings. 4 MEDIUM findings: 1 path traversal (mitigated by upstream controls) and 3 information disclosure via error messages in state. All are defense-in-depth recommendations, not exploitable vulnerabilities in current architecture.
